topic.what: Landmark review establishing that gut microbiota are 'mind-altering microorganisms' capable of influencing brain function, behavior, and stress responses via the microbiota-gut-brain axis, including HPA axis modulation, enteric nervous system signaling, vagal afferents, and immune pathways. Introduces the concept of psychobiotics.
topic.how: Synthesizes evidence from germ-free animal models, microbiota manipulation experiments (probiotics, antibiotics, colonization), and early human studies to build a bidirectional communication model between the gut microbiome and the CNS. Documents specific microbial metabolites (SCFA, tryptophan metabolites, GABA precursors) and neural pathways (vagus nerve, enteric NS) as mechanistic conduits. Proposes psychobiotics as a new functional category of probiotics with mental health applications.
topic.why_matters: Stress is a well-established trigger and exacerbator of gastritis and GI disease; the microbiota-gut-brain axis provides the biological mechanism by which psychological stress, microbiome composition, and gastric mucosal health are coupled. Relevant to understanding why chronic stress worsens gastritis, why anxiety and GI symptoms co-occur, and why microbiome-targeted interventions might have both GI and psychological benefits.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of distinct biological channels through which microbiota influence brain function
characteristics.value: High — vagal afferents, HPA axis, immune cytokines, tryptophan/serotonin metabolism, GABA, and SCFAs all documented
characteristics.vs_baseline: Earlier gut-brain research focused mainly on enteric nervous system as standalone system
characteristics.evidence: Germ-free animal models plus microbiota manipulation experiments across multiple laboratories
characteristics.context: Most mechanistic data from rodent models; human translation ongoing at time of publication

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which gut microbiome composition regulates stress hormone (cortisol/corticosterone) responses
characteristics.value: Strong in germ-free models — exaggerated stress response; colonization normalizes HPA axis reactivity in time-sensitive window
characteristics.vs_baseline: HPA axis regulation previously attributed entirely to CNS and adrenal feedback
characteristics.evidence: Germ-free vs. specific pathogen-free mouse comparisons; early colonization rescue experiments
characteristics.context: Critical period for microbiome-HPA axis programming appears to exist in early postnatal life

characteristics.what_it_measures: Originality of the psychobiotics framework as a new intervention category
characteristics.value: High — coined and operationally defined psychobiotics as probiotics with demonstrable CNS/mental health effects
characteristics.vs_baseline: Probiotics previously categorized only by GI or immune effects
characteristics.evidence: Conceptual framework supported by emerging preclinical and early human trial data
characteristics.context: Clinical evidence for psychobiotics was very early-stage in 2012; field has expanded substantially since

good_for: Constructing the biological rationale for stress-gastritis interactions mediated by the microbiome
Designing interventions that target gut-brain axis to improve both GI and psychological outcomes
Understanding why anxiety and functional GI disorders (including functional dyspepsia, IBS) co-occur
Framing psychobiotics as a research category for microbiome-mental health intervention studies
not_for: Clinical protocols for depression or anxiety treatment via probiotics — clinical evidence base was nascent in 2012
Gastritis-specific mechanistic claims without additional extrapolation from intestinal models
Replacing established psychiatric treatment with microbiome interventions
requires: Updated literature review — psychobiotics clinical trial landscape has expanded significantly since 2012
Awareness that most foundational data is from germ-free rodent models with uncertain human translation

novelty: Established the microbiota-gut-brain axis as a coherent bidirectional communication system with defined molecular and neural substrates
Coined and defined 'psychobiotics' as a functional category of live bacteria with demonstrable mental health effects
Demonstrated that HPA axis stress reactivity is programmed in part by early-life microbiome composition
Integrated immunological, neurochemical, and endocrine pathways into a unified gut-brain model
open_problems: Translation of germ-free animal findings to human microbiome-brain relationships remains incomplete
Specific psychobiotic strains, doses, and durations for defined mental health outcomes are not established
The critical window for microbiome programming of HPA axis function in humans is unknown
Mechanisms by which gastric microbiome (distinct from intestinal) influences brain function are unexplored
Whether microbiome-targeted interventions can reverse established HPA axis dysregulation in adults is unanswered
Interaction between stress-induced gastric acid changes and microbiome composition is a gap bridging this review and gastritis literature
